narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped—with a 44% chance of not passing. Picture it like a story the model pieced together from your profile: your Year 3 grades landing you in the bottom decile of your school stood out as the biggest red flag, whispering that the rigors of legal studies might prove an uphill battle just like they did then. Layered on top was your LSAT score of 31, which didn't quite shine bright enough to counter that, pulling the story firmly toward tougher odds. Even your solid Year 1 decile of 2 got overshadowed, as it hinted at early promise that faded over time. Your upper-middle family income, while a helpful foundation, didn't shift the narrative as strongly as we'd need in this mix, and your background as a Black male applicant added another thread of statistical challenge the model couldn't ignore. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.5 and full-time student status wove in more cautionary notes, suggesting the intensity ahead might stretch you thin. The one brighter spot was your gender, which nudged things slightly toward success in patterns we've seen before. Together, these pieces painted a picture of someone with heart and determination—your targeted passion for law is clear—but facing hurdles that have tripped up others before the bar. This isn't about your worth; it's a data-driven caution to build those academic foundations stronger first. You've got time to retake the LSAT, boost that GPA trajectory, or seek targeted prep—many have turned similar stories around. We're rooting for you to rewrite this ending. Keep pushing.
